preamble: The change from voiced labiodental fricative [v] to palatal glide [j] would require both a large shift in place of articulation (labiodental → palatal) and a manner change (fricative → approximant). No direct v→j change is attested as a regular sound change in any documented language family. The closest well-documented interaction is in cluster simplification: sequences of /v/ + /j/ (as in English 'view' /vjuː/) can be reduced to [j] through yod-absorption in casual speech, but this is a cluster reduction, not a standalone v→j rule. The preamble's claim that this 'is most likely before /i/ or /j/' is consistent with that reanalysis but not with a direct phonological change.

examples.note: In some varieties of informal English, the initial /vj/ sequence can reduce to [j] through yod-absorption. This is not a regular phonological rule but rather a casual speech reduction. It parallels the better-documented /tj/ → [tʃ] and /dj/ → [dʒ] patterns but produces glide output rather than an affricate. The original /v/ in 'view' is historically from Latin 'videre'.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English. Cambridge University Press.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
